english.title: English Literature BA
english.overview: English Literature is the close study of written texts across history, culture and genre — from medieval manuscripts to contemporary novels, from Renaissance drama to postcolonial poetry. The degree trains you to read carefully, think critically and write persuasively, equipping you with analytical tools drawn from literary theory, linguistics, history and philosophy. You will engage with canonical works while questioning the very idea of a canon, exploring who gets to tell stories and why. Graduates are recognised by employers across every sector for their ability to communicate complex ideas with clarity and precision.

yearByYear.focus: Foundations in literary reading and form
modules: Introduction to Literary Theory and Criticism
Medieval Literature: Chaucer and the Romance Tradition
Shakespeare and the Early Modern Stage
The Novel from Defoe to Austen
Poetry: Forms, Voices and Traditions
The Victorian Novel: Society, Empire and Self
Modernism and the Avant-Garde
Postcolonial Literature and Theory
American Literature from Whitman to Morrison
Gender, Sexuality and Literature
Contemporary Fiction and the Politics of Form
Life Writing: Autobiography, Memoir, Biography
Gothic and Horror: Aesthetics of Fear
Dissertation in English Literature

skills: Close textual analysis and the ability to construct arguments grounded in precise evidence from primary sources
Critical engagement with a wide range of theoretical frameworks including Marxist, feminist, postcolonial and psychoanalytic approaches
High-level academic writing — developing a distinct voice capable of handling complex, nuanced material
Research skills spanning archival work, literary databases and interdisciplinary sources
Seminar communication and the ability to listen, challenge and revise ideas in dialogue with others

professionalBodies.name: English Association
professionalBodies.benefit: Membership provides access to the journal English, teaching resources, and a network connecting academics, teachers and literary professionals across the UK

professionalBodies.name: Society of Authors
professionalBodies.benefit: Students and early-career graduates can join at reduced rates, gaining access to contract advice, emergency grants and a professional community for writers and editors

professionalBodies.name: Publishers Association
professionalBodies.benefit: The PA's graduate resources and entry-level schemes help English graduates navigate publishing careers, from editorial assistants to rights and marketing roles

english.employerPerspective: English graduates are consistently sought after by employers who need people who can read fast, think clearly and write well under pressure. In publishing, their analytical instincts and love of language are immediately applicable; in communications and consultancy, their ability to distil complex arguments into compelling prose distinguishes them from more technically focused peers. The dissertation, in particular, demonstrates self-direction and the capacity to manage a sustained, independent project.
english.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level, with English Literature (or English Language & Literature) usually required or strongly preferred. GCSE: English Language at grade 6/B or above expected. Some universities welcome applicants with English Language A-level alone; others require Literature. Contextual offers may be one to two grades lower at many institutions.
english.furtherStudy: MA English Literature, MA Creative Writing, MA Publishing, MA English Language Teaching (ELT), or PGCert/PGCE for teaching. PhD study is an option for those wishing to pursue an academic career. The Legal Practice Course (LPC) or Bar Professional Training Course (BPTC) are popular conversion routes for those interested in law.
isItForYou: Study this if you are passionate about reading widely and deeply, enjoy debating ideas and want a degree that develops transferable skills in critical thinking and communication valued across almost every profession
Study this if you are considering a career in publishing, journalism, teaching, law, policy or the arts and want a rigorous academic foundation with genuine flexibility
Think twice if you expect straightforward 'correct' answers — English Literature thrives on ambiguity and contested interpretation, and assessment is largely essay-based rather than quantitative

history.title: History BA
history.overview: History is the disciplined investigation of the human past — how societies changed, how power was exercised, how ordinary people lived and how the world came to be as it is. You will work across periods from ancient civilisations to the twenty-first century and across themes from gender and empire to economics and religion, always asking critical questions about evidence, causation and interpretation. The degree teaches you to weigh conflicting sources, construct logical arguments from incomplete information and communicate your conclusions with rigour and clarity — skills that transfer directly into law, journalism, policy, research and management. History graduates are consistently rated among the most employable humanities graduates by UK employers.

modules: The Historian's Craft: Sources, Methods and Debates
Medieval Europe: Society, Church and State
The Age of Revolutions, 1776–1848
Empire, Race and Colonial Encounter
Early Modern Britain: Reformation to Revolution
Twentieth-Century Europe: War, Genocide and Reconstruction
The British Empire and Decolonisation
Gender and the History of the Body
Cold War: Global Conflict and Ideology
Economic History: Industrialisation to Globalisation
History of Science, Medicine and Technology
Memory, Trauma and the Writing of History
Public History: Archives, Museums, Heritage
Dissertation in History

skills: Critical appraisal of primary and secondary sources — assessing reliability, context, bias and significance
Construction of evidence-based arguments in extended written form, with precise referencing and citation
Comparative analysis across different periods, geographies and social groups
Archival and digital research skills, including use of specialist historical databases and manuscript sources
Oral presentation and seminar debate — defending interpretations under intellectual scrutiny

professionalBodies.name: Royal Historical Society (RHS)
professionalBodies.benefit: Membership grants access to the RHS Bibliography — one of the most comprehensive historical research tools in the UK — plus grants, prizes and networking events for early-career historians

professionalBodies.name: Historical Association
professionalBodies.benefit: Particularly valuable for graduates pursuing teaching, the HA offers curriculum resources, the journal History, professional development events and a national network of local branches

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Historical Research (IHR)
professionalBodies.benefit: The IHR's seminars, fellowships and online resources support postgraduate and early-career researchers, and its library is the largest dedicated history library in the UK

careers.role: Civil Service Fast Stream / Policy Adviser

history.employerPerspective: History graduates bring something rare: the ability to synthesise large volumes of complex, contradictory information and produce a clear, persuasive account of what it means. In the Civil Service, that skill is fundamental to policy analysis and briefing ministers; in law firms, it underpins legal research and case construction. We find that History graduates adapt quickly, ask good questions and produce polished written work with relatively little guidance.
history.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; History is usually required or strongly preferred. GCSE: English Language at grade 5/C or above generally expected. Top universities (Oxford, Cambridge, LSE, UCL) may require AAA or A*AA. Contextual offers are available at many institutions.
history.furtherStudy: MA/MSc in History, International History, Public History, Heritage Studies, Archives and Records Management, or Area Studies. GDL (Graduate Diploma in Law) for legal conversion. PGCE for teaching. PhD for an academic career — funded places are available through the AHRC's doctoral training partnerships.
isItForYou: Study this if you are fascinated by why the world is the way it is, enjoy reading deeply and writing sustained analytical essays, and want a degree whose transferable skills open doors across law, government, media and business
Study this if you are considering a career in the Civil Service, law, journalism, heritage or education — History is consistently cited by employers in these sectors as excellent preparation

Think twice if you struggle with essay writing or find ambiguity frustrating — the vast majority of History assessment is written, and there are rarely simple or definitive answers to historical questions

philosophy.title: Philosophy BA
philosophy.overview: Philosophy is the systematic investigation of the most fundamental questions human beings face: What can we know, and how? What exists? What makes actions right or wrong? What is consciousness? What constitutes a valid argument? At university level, Philosophy is a rigorous analytical discipline that trains students to construct and evaluate arguments with unusual precision, to identify hidden assumptions and to think clearly about problems where the evidence is incomplete or contested. It is one of the most intellectually demanding and rewarding undergraduate degrees, consistently producing graduates who excel in law, finance, technology ethics, public policy and academia. Philosophy at the best UK universities — Oxford, Cambridge, UCL, St Andrews — is internationally recognised.

modules: Logic and Critical Reasoning
Introduction to Epistemology and Metaphysics
Moral Philosophy: Classical and Contemporary
Philosophy of Mind and Personal Identity
History of Philosophy: Plato and Aristotle
Philosophy of Language and Meaning
Political Philosophy: Justice, Liberty and Equality
Applied Ethics: Bioethics, AI and Environmental Philosophy
Continental Philosophy: Kant to Heidegger
Metaphysics: Time, Causation and Free Will
Philosophy of Science and Explanation
Ethics and the Law
Analytic Philosophy of Religion
Dissertation in Philosophy

skills: Formal and informal logical analysis — identifying valid and sound arguments, detecting fallacies and structuring proofs
Precise argumentative writing that states positions clearly, anticipates objections and responds to them with rigour
Conceptual analysis — breaking down complex ideas into their constituent components and testing their coherence
Critical synthesis of dense technical texts from multiple traditions and periods
Comfort with abstraction and the ability to apply abstract reasoning to concrete ethical and practical problems

professionalBodies.name: British Philosophical Association (BPA)
professionalBodies.benefit: The BPA supports UK philosophy students and early-career philosophers through conferences, bursaries, a mentoring scheme and resources promoting diversity and inclusion in the discipline

professionalBodies.name: Mind Association
professionalBodies.benefit: One of the leading philosophy associations in the world, publishing the journal Mind; membership provides access to the annual joint session conference and a community of professional philosophers

professionalBodies.name: Aristotelian Society
professionalBodies.benefit: One of the oldest philosophy societies in the world, its open meetings and symposia are accessible to students and its Supplementary Volumes provide cutting-edge philosophical research

careers.role: Policy Analyst / Civil Service Fast Stream
AI Ethics Researcher / Technology Policy Adviser

philosophy.employerPerspective: Philosophy graduates are among the most impressive critical thinkers we see. They have been trained to spot the weakness in an argument, to distinguish what is actually being claimed from what sounds plausible, and to communicate complex ideas with unusual concision. In strategy and consulting, those skills are directly applicable from day one. We have found that Philosophy graduates often outperform peers from more 'directly relevant' backgrounds within the first year because they know how to think, not just what to think.
philosophy.entryRequirements: Typical offer: A*AA–AAB at A-level; no specific subject required at most universities, though Mathematics or a humanity is often preferred. Top universities (Oxford A*AA, Cambridge A*AA, UCL AAA) are highly competitive. GCSE: strong grades broadly expected. Philosophy A-level is not normally required and is offered at relatively few schools.
philosophy.furtherStudy: BPhil, MPhil or MA in Philosophy, Philosophy of Mind, Logic, Ethics, Political Philosophy or Philosophy of Science. GDL for law conversion. MSc in Computer Science or Artificial Intelligence (philosophy graduates are well-placed for AI ethics and safety research). PhD in Philosophy for academic careers — AHRC-funded places available.
isItForYou: Study this if you are energised by abstract problems, enjoy reading dense theoretical texts closely and want a degree that develops the clearest possible thinking — skills that translate into law, AI ethics, policy and consulting
Study this if you have strong A-level results and enjoy the challenge of holding multiple conflicting views in mind simultaneously before reaching a carefully reasoned conclusion

Think twice if you need immediate vocational focus or find purely argumentative, essay-based assessment demotivating — Philosophy rarely has 'right' answers and rewards those who are genuinely interested in the questions for their own sake

classics.title: Classics BA
classics.overview: Classics is the interdisciplinary study of the ancient Greek and Roman worlds — their languages, literature, history, philosophy, art, archaeology and reception in later culture. It is one of the oldest university disciplines in Britain and remains intellectually formidable: students who pursue the full linguistic route will learn Latin and Ancient Greek to a high level while simultaneously studying the thought of Plato and Aristotle, the poetry of Homer and Virgil, and the political history of Athens and Rome. The degree is also offered in 'civilisation' routes that do not require prior language study, making it accessible without school-level Latin or Greek. Classics graduates are prized for their intellectual range, linguistic precision and the depth of their engagement with foundational Western thought.

modules: Latin Prose and Poetry: Cicero and Virgil
Ancient Greek Language: Homer and Plato
Greek History: From Polis to Empire
Roman History: Republic and Principate
Classical Mythology and Its Receptions
Greek Tragedy: Aeschylus, Sophocles and Euripides
Roman Elegy and Lyric: Catullus, Propertius, Ovid
Ancient Philosophy: Plato's Republic and Aristotle's Ethics
Archaeology of the Greek World
Gender and Sexuality in the Ancient World
The Roman Novel: Petronius and Apuleius
Classical Reception: Shakespeare, Opera, Film
Ancient Historiography: Thucydides and Tacitus
Dissertation in Classics

skills: Translation from Latin and/or Ancient Greek, demonstrating close attention to syntactic structure and lexical nuance
Interdisciplinary analytical thinking — integrating linguistic, historical, archaeological and philosophical evidence
Extended argumentative writing at a high academic standard, drawing on primary texts in their original languages
Detailed knowledge of foundational Western philosophical and political thought
Research synthesis across multiple disciplines and scholarly traditions

professionalBodies.name: Classical Association
professionalBodies.benefit: The oldest classics body in the UK, offering student membership, the journal Greece & Rome, the annual conference, grants and a vibrant network for academics, teachers and enthusiasts

professionalBodies.name: Society for the Promotion of Hellenic Studies
professionalBodies.benefit: Membership includes access to the Journal of Hellenic Studies, the Hellenic Society library in London and bursaries for Greek travel and research

professionalBodies.name: Roman Society
professionalBodies.benefit: Offers student membership, access to the Journal of Roman Studies, travel grants and networking with professional classicists and archaeologists

classics.employerPerspective: Classics graduates are exceptional communicators and analytical thinkers. The discipline demands mastery of two difficult languages, command of a vast body of literature and the ability to reason across history, philosophy and culture simultaneously — a combination that produces outstanding barristers, policy advisers and consultants. We look specifically for Classics graduates when filling roles that require both rigorous logical thinking and the ability to write beautifully under pressure.
classics.entryRequirements: Typical offer: A*AA–AAB at A-level. Top universities (Oxford A*AA, Cambridge A*AA) are among the most competitive in the country. For language routes, prior Latin or Ancient Greek is normally required, often at GCSE level. Civilisation routes are accessible without prior language study. GCSE: strong grades expected broadly; Latin and/or Ancient Greek GCSE is a strong advantage.
classics.furtherStudy: MPhil/MA in Classics, Ancient History, Classical Archaeology, Classical Reception, or Ancient Philosophy. PGCE for teaching Latin or Ancient History. GDL for law. PhD for an academic career — AHRC doctoral training partnerships fund competitive places at leading UK universities.
isItForYou: Study this if you are drawn to the intellectual origins of Western thought, enjoy the challenge of learning ancient languages and want a degree that combines literary, philosophical, historical and archaeological perspectives in a single programme

Study this if you have strong academic ability and are open to careers in law, the Civil Service, heritage or academia — Classics is consistently among the degrees associated with the highest graduate earnings relative to tuition investment

Think twice if you find language learning frustrating or prefer a more directly vocational degree — while civilisation routes offer some flexibility, the full Classics degree is demanding and requires genuine commitment to linguistic mastery

modernLanguages.title: Modern Languages BA
modernLanguages.overview: Modern Languages is the study of one or two living languages to a high level of proficiency alongside their literatures, cultures, histories and societies. The degree is genuinely transformative: students typically spend their third year abroad as a student, language assistant or intern in a country where their language is spoken, achieving near-native fluency in reading, writing, speaking and listening. Alongside language acquisition, you will study literature from the Renaissance to the present day, film, cultural theory and contemporary society in the target country or countries. Modern Languages graduates are among the most internationally mobile of all UK graduates and are recruited into finance, diplomacy, the Civil Service, publishing, translation and global business.

modules: Advanced Language Skills I: Grammar and Communication
Introduction to Literary Study in the Target Language
Cultures and Societies of the Target Language World
Translation: Theory and Practice
Film and Visual Culture in the Target Language
Advanced Language Skills II: Academic and Professional Register
Literature from the Enlightenment to Modernism
Contemporary Writing and Postcolonial Voices
Media, Politics and Society in the Target Language World
Interpreting: Consecutive and Liaison Skills
Year Abroad (university exchange, language assistantship or work placement)
Reflective Language Portfolio
Independent Research Project (year abroad country)

skills: Near-native written and spoken proficiency in one or two modern languages, including formal, academic and professional registers
Cross-cultural competence — understanding how language shapes identity, politics and society in different national contexts
Translation and interpreting skills, working between at least two languages in both written and oral modes
Close literary analysis in a second language, with sensitivity to linguistic nuance and cultural context
Adaptability and resilience developed through extended independent living in a foreign country

professionalBodies.name: Chartered Institute of Linguists (CIOL)
professionalBodies.benefit: CIOL membership and its Diploma in Translation (DipTrans) is the UK's leading professional qualification for translators and interpreters, highly valued by employers in language services

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Translation & Interpreting (ITI)
professionalBodies.benefit: ITI membership gives access to professional development, a searchable directory listing, specialist networks by language and subject, and codes of professional conduct for practising translators

professionalBodies.name: Association for Language Learning (ALL)
professionalBodies.benefit: Particularly useful for graduates pursuing teaching, ALL provides curriculum resources, the journal Language Learning Journal and a professional community for modern language educators

modernLanguages.employerPerspective: Modern Languages graduates offer something that cannot easily be developed in professional life: genuine bilingual or trilingual fluency combined with real cultural intelligence. In international banking, diplomacy and global business, the ability to read a contract, negotiate a deal or conduct research in another language without relying on a translator is a significant competitive advantage. Year-abroad experience also tells us a great deal about a candidate's self-reliance and adaptability — qualities we prize as much as language skills themselves.
modernLanguages.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; the target language(s) at A-level normally required, ideally at grade A or B. Native speakers of the target language may be redirected to different language combinations. GCSE: English at grade 5/C or above expected. Some universities offer beginners' language options in one of the languages studied.
modernLanguages.furtherStudy: MA in Translation, Interpreting, Applied Linguistics, International Relations, European Studies or Area Studies. PGCE for teaching. Professional qualifications: CIOL DipTrans or ITI membership. MA/MSc in International Business or Global Affairs for those moving toward business. PhD for academic careers.
isItForYou: Study this if you love languages and are excited by the prospect of achieving genuine fluency, spending a year abroad and building a career with genuine international mobility across diplomacy, business, translation or journalism

Study this if you are drawn to the cultures, literatures and political histories of other countries and want a degree that opens doors to international organisations, the Foreign Office and global companies

Think twice if you are not prepared to commit fully to the year abroad or if your language motivation is primarily instrumental — the degree works best when driven by genuine curiosity about other cultures and a willingness to step outside your comfort zone

french.title: French BA
french.overview: French is one of the world's most studied languages and the official language of 29 countries across five continents, making it an exceptionally valuable degree for anyone seeking an international career. At university, you will study the language to an advanced level across all four skills — reading, writing, listening and speaking — while exploring French and Francophone literature from the seventeenth century to the present, French cinema, cultural theory and contemporary politics in France and the wider Francophone world. The compulsory year in a French-speaking country is a defining feature of the degree: it builds near-native fluency and cross-cultural confidence that remain with graduates throughout their professional lives. French is widely studied in combination with Spanish, German, linguistics, history or law.

yearByYear.focus: Advanced French language and cultural foundations
modules: Advanced French Grammar and Written Expression
Introduction to French Literature: Molière to Baudelaire
French Oral Communication and Phonetics
History of France: Revolution to the Fifth Republic
Translation from and into French
Twentieth-Century French Literature: Proust, Sartre, Beauvoir
Francophone Literature: Africa, the Caribbean and Québec
French Cinema: From the Nouvelle Vague to the Present
Advanced French Writing and Professional Communication
French Political Thought and Contemporary Society

yearByYear.focus: Year abroad in a French-speaking country
modules: Year Abroad (university exchange or language assistantship in France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada or Francophone Africa)
French Language Portfolio: Advanced Register
Research Project on Francophone Theme

skills: Near-native written and spoken proficiency in French, including formal, literary, journalistic and professional registers
Close literary analysis of French and Francophone texts, with sensitivity to historical and postcolonial contexts
Translation between English and French across a range of text types, from literary prose to legal and commercial documents
Cultural and intercultural competence in French and Francophone contexts across Europe, Africa and the Americas
Autonomous language learning strategies developed through the year abroad

professionalBodies.name: Chartered Institute of Linguists (CIOL)
professionalBodies.benefit: The CIOL's Diploma in Translation (DipTrans) for French is the benchmark professional qualification for French translators in the UK and is recognised by the EU institutions and international bodies

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Translation & Interpreting (ITI)
professionalBodies.benefit: ITI membership and its French language network provide professional development, a client directory listing, specialist subject networks and access to the European Language Industry Survey

professionalBodies.name: Association for Language Learning (ALL)
professionalBodies.benefit: For graduates moving into French teaching, ALL provides teaching resources, the Language Learning Journal, CPD events and a professional community for modern language educators

french.employerPerspective: French remains among the most commercially valuable European languages for UK businesses with operations in Europe, Africa and Canada. Candidates with degree-level French — particularly those who have lived in a French-speaking country — bring fluency and cultural understanding that takes years to develop any other way. In financial services, the ability to work directly with French-speaking clients and colleagues in Paris, Geneva or Luxembourg is a tangible competitive advantage that we factor into hiring decisions.
french.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; French A-level normally required, ideally at grade A. Native French speakers are usually directed to different language combinations or joint degrees. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. Some universities offer an intensive beginners' option for students combining French with another subject where they are a beginner.
french.furtherStudy: MA in Translation Studies, French Studies, Francophone Studies, Applied Linguistics or European Politics. PGCE for French teaching. CIOL DipTrans (French) for professional translation. MA or MSc in International Business or European Law for business-oriented careers. PhD for academic research.
isItForYou: Study this if you have a genuine love of French language and Francophone cultures, are excited by the prospect of near-native fluency and a year in France or another French-speaking country, and want a degree that opens doors in Europe, Africa and internationally

Study this if you are considering translation, teaching, diplomacy, international business or journalism — French remains one of the world's five most important working languages and is the second most studied language in the world

Think twice if your French is already at a high level but you are motivated purely instrumentally — the degree involves deep literary and cultural study alongside language acquisition, and those who thrive are genuinely curious about France and the Francophone world

spanish.title: Spanish BA
spanish.overview: Spanish is the second most widely spoken native language in the world, with over 480 million speakers across Spain, Latin America, the United States and beyond, making a Spanish degree one of the most globally marketable qualifications available. At university, you will develop advanced proficiency across all language skills — including the ability to navigate the rich regional variation between Castilian Spanish and the major Latin American varieties — alongside deep study of Spanish and Latin American literature, film, cultural history and politics. The year abroad, spent in Spain or a Latin American country, is a transformative experience that produces true bilingual confidence and cultural intelligence valued by employers across finance, business, law and the creative industries.

yearByYear.focus: Advanced Spanish language and Iberian contexts
modules: Advanced Spanish Grammar and Composition
Introduction to Spanish Literature: Golden Age to Realism
Spain and Its Histories: Monarchy, Republic, Civil War
Translation: English into Spanish and Spanish into English
Oral Spanish: Accent, Register and Discourse

yearByYear.focus: Latin American literatures and advanced language
modules: The Latin American Boom: García Márquez, Borges, Cortázar
Spanish Cinema: Buñuel, Almodóvar and Contemporary Film
Postcolonial Latin America: Society, Politics and Culture
Advanced Spanish Writing: Academic and Professional Texts
Language Variation: Dialectology and Sociolinguistics

yearByYear.focus: Year abroad in a Spanish-speaking country
modules: Year Abroad (university exchange or language assistantship in Spain, Mexico, Argentina, Colombia or other Spanish-speaking country)
Spanish Language Portfolio: Advanced and Professional Register
Independent Research Project on Hispanic Theme

skills: Near-native written and spoken proficiency in Spanish across Castilian and major Latin American varieties
Literary and cultural analysis of texts from Spain and Latin America, with attention to postcolonial and subaltern perspectives
Translation between English and Spanish in a range of registers, including literary, commercial and legal texts
Intercultural competence across diverse Spanish-speaking contexts in Europe, Latin America and the United States
Sustained independent research, developed through the dissertation and year-abroad project

professionalBodies.name: Chartered Institute of Linguists (CIOL)
professionalBodies.benefit: The CIOL's Diploma in Translation (DipTrans) for Spanish is the benchmark professional qualification for Spanish translators in the UK, recognised by courts, government bodies and international organisations

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Translation & Interpreting (ITI)
professionalBodies.benefit: ITI's Spanish language network provides professional development events, specialist subject groups, a client directory and the annual ITI conference connecting language professionals across the sector

professionalBodies.name: Association for Language Learning (ALL)
professionalBodies.benefit: For graduates entering Spanish teaching, ALL offers CPD resources, the Language Learning Journal, a teachers' network and advocacy for modern languages in the UK curriculum

careers.role: Latin America Business Development / Export Manager

spanish.employerPerspective: Spanish is one of the fastest-growing major languages in global business, particularly in financial services, technology and commodities where Latin American markets are increasingly significant. Graduates who can work fluently in Spanish and navigate cultural differences between Spain and the diverse Latin American countries are genuinely rare and in demand. We find that Spanish graduates from UK universities bring analytical rigour alongside their language skills — they are not just translators but business thinkers who happen to be bilingual.
spanish.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; Spanish A-level normally required, ideally at grade A or B. Native or heritage Spanish speakers are usually redirected to different combinations. Some universities offer beginners' Spanish for students combining it with another language or subject. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected.
spanish.furtherStudy: MA in Translation, Spanish and Latin American Studies, Hispanic Studies, Applied Linguistics, International Business or Latin American Studies. PGCE for Spanish teaching. CIOL DipTrans (Spanish). MBA for business-oriented graduates. PhD for academic research in Hispanic Studies or comparative literature.
isItForYou: Study this if you are passionate about the Spanish language and its cultural diversity across Spain, Latin America and beyond, and want a degree that produces genuine bilingual fluency alongside deep literary and cultural knowledge

Study this if you are drawn to international careers in business, diplomacy, translation, journalism or development — Spanish is the official language of 20 countries and one of the six official UN languages

Think twice if you are looking for a vocational qualification with a direct professional route — Spanish opens many doors but requires you to be proactive about combining the degree with internships, further qualifications or professional experience

german.title: German BA
german.overview: German is the most widely spoken native language in Europe and the official language of Germany, Austria and Switzerland — three of Europe's most economically significant countries. A German degree develops advanced language proficiency alongside deep engagement with German-speaking literatures, cultural history, philosophy and contemporary European politics. You will read Goethe and Kafka in the original, engage with twentieth-century German history including the Holocaust and GDR, study the philosophical tradition from Kant through Nietzsche to the Frankfurt School, and spend a year in a German-speaking country developing near-native fluency and professional confidence. German is widely studied in combination with business, philosophy, law or another modern language.

yearByYear.focus: Advanced German language and cultural foundations
modules: Advanced German Grammar and Written Skills
German Literature from Classicism to Expressionism: Goethe, Schiller, Kafka
History of the German-Speaking World: 1848 to the Present
German Oral and Aural Skills: Register and Pronunciation
Translation: German into English and English into German
German Literature After 1945: Brecht, Böll, Sebald
German Cinema: Weimar, Third Reich and New German Cinema
The Frankfurt School: Critical Theory from Adorno to Habermas
GDR Literature and Culture: Living Under Socialism
Advanced German Writing and Academic Communication

yearByYear.focus: Year abroad in a German-speaking country
modules: Year Abroad (university exchange or language assistantship in Germany, Austria or Switzerland)
German Language Portfolio: Professional and Academic Register
Research Project on German or Austrian Theme

skills: Near-native proficiency in written and spoken German, including academic, professional and colloquial registers
Literary and cultural analysis of German-language texts from the Enlightenment to the contemporary, with attention to historical and philosophical context
Translation between English and German across a wide range of text types, including literary, technical and commercial documents
Deep engagement with German intellectual traditions in philosophy, critical theory and political thought
Cross-cultural adaptability developed through extended residence in a German-speaking country

professionalBodies.name: Chartered Institute of Linguists (CIOL)
professionalBodies.benefit: The CIOL's Diploma in Translation (DipTrans) for German is the gold-standard qualification for German translators in the UK, particularly valued in technical and legal sectors where German is widely used

professionalBodies.name: Institute of Translation & Interpreting (ITI)
professionalBodies.benefit: ITI's German language and specialist technical networks provide professional development, market guidance and a searchable directory for practising German translators and interpreters

professionalBodies.name: German-British Chamber of Industry and Commerce (GBCCI)
professionalBodies.benefit: The GBCCI provides networking opportunities, business events and links to German-owned companies operating in the UK — a valuable resource for graduates moving into German-UK business contexts

careers.role: European Policy Analyst / EU Affairs Officer

german.employerPerspective: German remains the prestige language of European business in manufacturing, engineering, finance and pharmaceutical sectors. Companies with significant German operations — and there are many in the UK — value candidates who can work across cultural and linguistic lines. German graduates also tend to have excellent technical precision: the language itself rewards exactness, and graduates often bring an unusual clarity of thought and expression to analytical work.
german.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; German A-level normally required, ideally at grade A or B. Some universities offer an intensive beginners' route for students studying German alongside another language where they are more advanced. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. German is one of the few languages where universities actively encourage beginners, recognising declining uptake at school.
german.furtherStudy: MA in German Studies, Translation Studies, Applied Linguistics, European Politics, International Business or Central European Studies. PGCE for German teaching. CIOL DipTrans (German). MBA or MSc for business roles. PhD for academic careers in German literature, philosophy or cultural studies.
isItForYou: Study this if you are drawn to Germany's rich philosophical, literary and cultural heritage, want to achieve near-native German fluency, and see yourself working in European business, translation, teaching or international affairs

Study this if you are interested in philosophy, intellectual history or contemporary European politics — the German intellectual tradition is central to the discipline and opens doors to advanced study in these areas

Think twice if you are already studying two languages and struggling to manage the workload — while German is extremely valuable, the degree works best when you have genuine enthusiasm for German culture and thought rather than purely adding a third language to your CV

linguistics.title: Linguistics BA
linguistics.overview: Linguistics is the scientific study of language: how it is structured, how it is acquired, how it varies across communities and changes over time, how it is processed in the brain and how it shapes social identity and power. It is a fascinatingly interdisciplinary subject, drawing on psychology, computer science, anthropology, sociology and neuroscience alongside the formal analysis of grammar and sound. Students learn to apply rigorous analytical methods — including phonemic transcription, syntactic tree diagrams and corpus analysis — to the full diversity of human languages, from Mandarin to Arabic to languages of the Amazon. Linguistics is particularly relevant for careers in speech and language therapy (with a relevant Masters), natural language processing, teaching English as a foreign language and computational linguistics.

modules: Introduction to Phonetics and Phonology
Morphology and Syntax: How Language is Structured
Semantics and Pragmatics
Language Acquisition: First and Second Language
The World's Languages: Diversity and Universals
Sociolinguistics: Language, Identity and Power
Historical Linguistics: Language Change and Reconstruction
Psycholinguistics and the Language Brain
Corpus Linguistics and Computational Methods
Discourse Analysis and Pragmatics
Natural Language Processing and Computational Linguistics
Language, Gender and Sexuality
Clinical Linguistics and Communication Disorders
Dissertation in Linguistics

skills: Phonemic transcription using the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) and analysis of sound systems across languages
Syntactic and morphological analysis — constructing phrase structure trees and analysing grammatical variation
Corpus analysis using specialist software (AntConc, Sketch Engine) to identify patterns in large language datasets
Experimental design and data analysis for psycholinguistic and sociolinguistic research
Ability to analyse language objectively across registers, dialects and languages without normative or prescriptive bias

professionalBodies.name: Linguistics Association of Great Britain (LAGB)
professionalBodies.benefit: The LAGB holds annual conferences, publishes the journal Transactions of the Philological Society and offers student bursaries and a mentoring network for early-career linguists

professionalBodies.name: Royal College of Speech & Language Therapists (RCSLT)
professionalBodies.benefit: Linguistics graduates wishing to pursue Speech and Language Therapy will need an RCSLT-accredited postgraduate degree; the RCSLT provides information on routes into the profession and CPD for qualified therapists

professionalBodies.name: British Association of Applied Linguistics (BAAL)
professionalBodies.benefit: BAAL supports applied research in language education, language policy and sociolinguistics, with an annual conference, the journal Applied Linguistics and travel grants for student researchers

careers.role: Natural Language Processing (NLP) Engineer / Computational Linguist
Speech and Language Therapist (via accredited Masters)

linguistics.employerPerspective: Linguistics graduates have become increasingly important in the tech sector as conversational AI and natural language processing move from research curiosity to core product. The ability to analyse language structure formally — and to think about meaning, ambiguity and context with precision — maps directly onto the challenges we face in building reliable language models. Beyond tech, linguistics graduates in teaching and clinical roles bring a unique ability to diagnose language difficulties and design effective interventions that purely education-focused graduates often lack.
linguistics.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–BBB at A-level; no specific subject required at most universities, though English Language, a modern language, or Mathematics is often noted as advantageous. Linguistics A-level is offered at relatively few schools but is warmly welcomed where available. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. Some programmes are joint with a language (e.g. Linguistics and French) and may require that language at A-level.
linguistics.furtherStudy: MSc in Computational Linguistics, Natural Language Processing, Speech and Language Therapy (pre-registration), Applied Linguistics, TESOL or Language Acquisition. PGCE for teaching. PhD in any area of linguistics — ESRC doctoral training partnerships fund quantitative and mixed-methods linguistic research. MSc Computer Science (conversion) for graduates moving into NLP development.
isItForYou: Study this if you are fascinated by how language actually works — not just in English, but across the extraordinary diversity of human languages — and are drawn to analytical methods, data and the intersection of language with psychology, society and technology

Study this if you are considering a career in speech and language therapy, computational linguistics, language teaching or user experience research, where formal training in language structure is a genuine professional asset

Think twice if you expect to focus primarily on literature or creative writing — Linguistics is a science as much as a humanity, involving formal analysis, IPA transcription and quantitative methods, and it suits students who enjoy systematic investigation

artHistory.title: History of Art BA
artHistory.overview: History of Art is the study of visual culture across time and place — painting, sculpture, architecture, photography, film, design and digital media — analysed through historical, cultural, political and theoretical lenses. It is a discipline that asks not just 'what does this look like?' but 'who made it, for whom, why, and what did it mean in its time and context?' Students develop visual literacy of the highest order: the ability to look carefully, to describe precisely and to interpret the formal and iconographic content of works of art with scholarly rigour. The degree is intellectually cosmopolitan, requiring engagement with non-Western art histories, feminist and postcolonial critiques of the canon, and contemporary theory. History of Art graduates are highly sought after in the creative industries, heritage sector, auction houses and arts administration.

modules: Looking and Describing: Methods in Art History
From Antiquity to the Renaissance: Art in the Western Tradition
Art of the Nineteenth Century: Realism, Impressionism, Symbolism
Architecture: Theory, History and Space
Non-Western Art Histories: Africa, Asia, the Americas
Modernism and the Avant-Garde: Cubism to Abstract Expressionism
Art Theory and Critical Methods: Formalism, Psychoanalysis, Semiotics
Photography and the Image: From Daguerre to Digital
Postcolonial Art Histories and Global Contemporary Art
Museum Studies: Display, Collection and Interpretation
Contemporary Art after 1989: The Global Art World
Women Artists and Feminist Art History
The Art Market: History, Ethics and Commerce
Dissertation in History of Art

skills: Visual analysis — the ability to describe and interpret formal qualities (composition, colour, technique, scale) with scholarly precision
Iconographic and contextual analysis, situating works of art within their historical, social and political contexts
Engagement with a wide range of critical and theoretical frameworks including feminism, psychoanalysis, postcolonialism and semiotics
Museum and archival research skills, including study of original works, collection catalogues and archival documents
Persuasive written and oral communication about visual material, developed through essays, presentations and curated displays

professionalBodies.name: Association for Art History
professionalBodies.benefit: The UK's leading body for art historians, offering student membership, the journal Art History, the annual conference, careers resources and a lively network connecting academics and museum professionals

professionalBodies.name: Museums Association (MA)
professionalBodies.benefit: Membership provides access to the journal Museums Journal, the annual conference, a mentoring scheme and professional development resources — essential for graduates moving into curatorial and museum management roles

professionalBodies.name: Chartered Institute for Archaeologists (CIfA)
professionalBodies.benefit: For graduates moving into heritage and built environment roles alongside art history, CIfA accreditation supports careers in conservation, heritage management and listed building work

artHistory.employerPerspective: History of Art graduates bring something we find hard to develop in people who come from other backgrounds: genuine visual intelligence, the ability to look at an object and construct a compelling, scholarly argument about its significance. In the auction house world, that skill is the foundation of everything — valuation, provenance research, client advice. In museums, curators who understand not just what an object is but why it matters visually and culturally make exhibitions that genuinely engage audiences. We hire History of Art graduates wherever we can.
artHistory.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; no specific subjects required at most universities, though History of Art (where offered), History, English, a modern language or Fine Art are often seen in successful applicants. History of Art A-level is offered at relatively few schools. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. Portfolio not normally required for History of Art (as opposed to Fine Art).
artHistory.furtherStudy: MA in History of Art, Curatorship, Museum Studies, Arts Administration, Conservation, or Auction House and Art Market Studies. PGCE for teaching Art History (limited uptake). PhD for academic careers. Postgraduate diplomas at Christie's Education and Sotheby's Institute of Art are highly regarded in the art market world.
isItForYou: Study this if you are genuinely excited by looking at art, architecture and visual culture, enjoy connecting objects to their historical and social context and want a career in museums, galleries, the art market or arts administration

Study this if you have strong essay-writing skills and enjoy theory — History of Art involves dense readings in art theory, critical studies and cultural history, and rewards those who engage enthusiastically with intellectual frameworks

Think twice if you are primarily interested in making art rather than analysing it — this is an academic discipline, not a studio practice, and applicants who confuse the two often find the theoretical demands of the degree unexpected

musicHumanities.title: Music BA/BMus
musicHumanities.overview: Music at university level is far broader than school-level music suggests: alongside performance and composition, you will study music theory and analysis, music history from the medieval period to the present, ethnomusicology, music technology and the cultural, social and political contexts in which music is made and heard. UK university Music departments range from conservatoire-style programmes emphasising performance to more academic departments where historical musicology and analytical skills take priority, so it is important to research individual programmes carefully. Graduates are trained musicians, thinkers and communicators who go on to performance careers, music education, the music industry, broadcasting, therapy and arts administration.

modules: Harmony, Counterpoint and Tonal Analysis
Music History I: Medieval, Renaissance and Baroque
Aural Training and Sight-Reading
Performance Studies: Solo and Ensemble
Introduction to Ethnomusicology
Music Since 1900: Modernism, Minimalism, Spectralism
Music and Society: Politics, Race and Gender in Music
Composition: Acoustic and Electronic Techniques
Music Technology: Recording, Production and Sound Design
Music Psychology and Cognitive Science of Music
Advanced Performance or Composition Portfolio
World Music: Traditions, Hybridity and Globalisation
The Music Industry: Commerce, Copyright and Careers
Dissertation in Music (Musicology or Composition)

skills: Score reading and harmonic analysis — identifying structural, harmonic and stylistic features in music from multiple periods and traditions
Aural skills: transcription, sight-singing, and the ability to listen analytically to complex musical textures
Compositional technique in acoustic and/or electronic media, drawing on a range of historical and contemporary models
Critical writing about music — situating works in their historical, cultural and theoretical contexts with scholarly precision
Performance skills in a principal study, developed through individual tuition and ensemble participation

professionalBodies.name: Incorporated Society of Musicians (ISM)
professionalBodies.benefit: The ISM is the UK's professional body for musicians, offering student and graduate membership, legal support, CPD, a jobs board and advocacy for music in education — essential for all career paths in music

professionalBodies.name: Associated Board of the Royal Schools of Music (ABRSM)
professionalBodies.benefit: ABRSM examiners and assessors are largely drawn from Music graduates; its teaching qualifications (DipABRSM, LRSM) provide a structured route into instrumental teaching for performance-focused graduates

professionalBodies.name: British Academy of Songwriters, Composers and Authors (BASCA / IVORS Academy)
professionalBodies.benefit: The Ivors Academy represents songwriters and composers, providing royalty guidance, industry connections, awards and advocacy — essential for graduates working in composition and the music industry

musicHumanities.employerPerspective: Music graduates are among our most versatile recruits — they have trained both analytically and practically, can work under pressure to tight deadlines and bring a level of focused attention to detail that we prize across roles from artist management to music supervision. The dissertation demonstrates sustained independent research; the performance portfolio shows commitment and the ability to communicate emotionally through a medium other than words. We look for Music graduates when we need people who combine analytical rigour with genuine creative instinct.
musicHumanities.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; Music A-level normally required, and Grade 7–8 ABRSM (or equivalent) on a principal study instrument or voice. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. Music theory — ideally Grade 5 or above — is expected at most departments. Auditions or performance showcases are required at conservatoire-style programmes and some university departments. A-level Music Technology may be accepted in place of or alongside A-level Music at some institutions.
musicHumanities.furtherStudy: MMus in Performance, Composition, Musicology, Ethnomusicology, Music Technology or Music Therapy (professional registration route). PGCE for school Music teaching. PhD for academic careers. Postgraduate diplomas at the Royal Academy of Music, Royal College of Music or Guildhall School of Music for performance-focused routes.
isItForYou: Study this if music is a genuine passion — you read scores, practise regularly, listen widely across genres and want to develop your analytical and creative musical skills in an intellectually rigorous environment

Study this if you are open to a wide range of career outcomes: Music graduates go into performance, teaching, therapy, the music industry, broadcasting and general graduate careers in far greater numbers than any single pathway

Think twice if performance is your only goal — a conservatoire programme may suit you better, as university Music degrees balance academic and practical elements and the academic component (essays, dissertations, historical analysis) is substantial

englishCreativeWriting.title: English & Creative Writing BA
englishCreativeWriting.overview: English & Creative Writing combines the close literary reading and critical analysis of an English Literature degree with practical training in the craft of writing fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, script and other forms. This dual approach — reading as a writer and writing as a reader — produces graduates who understand literature both from the inside (as makers) and the outside (as critics), developing a sophisticated creative voice alongside genuine scholarly rigour. The degree is not simply about being 'given space to write': workshops involve detailed, peer-led critique, and craft modules address the technical challenges of point of view, voice, structure, compression and revision. Graduates go into writing, publishing, journalism, education, arts administration and the many creative sectors that value clear, distinctive communication.

yearByYear.focus: Reading as a writer, writing as a reader
modules: The Craft of Fiction: Point of View, Voice and Structure
Poetry Workshop: Forms, Compression and the Line
Reading Contemporary Literature: The Novel Since 2000
Literary Nonfiction: Memoir, Essay and the Personal Voice
Shakespeare and the Early Modern Stage
Advanced Fiction Workshop: Revision and the Long Form
Screenwriting and Dramatic Writing
Postcolonial and World Literature
The Short Story: Carver, Munro, Borges
Literature and Theory: Narrative, Genre and the Reader
The Writer in the World: Careers, Publishing and the Literary Marketplace
Gothic and Speculative Fiction Workshop
Life Writing: Autobiography, Memoir, Biography
Creative Dissertation: Extended Writing Project with Critical Reflection

skills: Creative writing craft: structuring narrative, controlling voice and point of view, writing dialogue, revising and editing extended pieces
Close critical reading of literary texts, with the ability to analyse how formal and stylistic choices produce meaning
Constructive peer critique — giving and receiving detailed, honest feedback in a workshop setting
Genre awareness: understanding the conventions of fiction, poetry, non-fiction and script and how contemporary writers work with and against those conventions
Self-editing and revision strategies — the discipline of returning to a draft, identifying weaknesses and making them strengths

professionalBodies.name: Society of Authors
professionalBodies.benefit: Graduate and student members gain access to contract vetting, emergency financial assistance, the Members Update newsletter and a vibrant professional community of published writers and editors

professionalBodies.name: National Association of Writers in Education (NAWE)
professionalBodies.benefit: NAWE supports writers who work in educational settings, offering a professional directory, CPD, the journal Writing in Education and advocacy for creative writing as a discipline in higher and further education

professionalBodies.name: Publishers Association
professionalBodies.benefit: PA resources and entry-level publishing schemes are particularly relevant for Creative Writing graduates moving into editorial, agenting, rights and marketing roles in the publishing industry

careers.salary: £15,000–£25,000 early career / highly variable at 5 years

englishCreativeWriting.employerPerspective: Creative Writing graduates who have also studied English Literature bring something genuinely unusual: they are technically skilled writers who have also read deeply and thought critically about how literature works. In publishing, we need editorial assistants who can read a manuscript and immediately identify what is wrong with its structure or voice — Creative Writing graduates can do that because they have been trained to think about writing from the inside. In content and communications, their ability to find a fresh angle, write with personality and revise efficiently is immediately evident.
englishCreativeWriting.entryRequirements: Typical offer: AAB–ABB at A-level; English Literature and/or English Language usually required or strongly preferred. Some universities request a writing sample or portfolio as part of the admissions process. GCSE: English Language at grade 6/B or above expected given the writing demands of the degree. Contextual offers available at many institutions.
englishCreativeWriting.furtherStudy: MA in Creative Writing (widely available at UK universities including UEA, Goldsmiths, Warwick, Bath Spa and Manchester — UEA is the oldest and most prestigious). MA in Publishing, Journalism, Screenwriting or Children's Book Illustration. PGCE for English teaching. Curtis Brown Creative and Faber Academy run popular postgraduate-level programmes outside the university system.
isItForYou: Study this if you are genuinely committed to writing — not just enjoying reading, but to the practice of writing and rewriting with discipline — and want both creative development and critical grounding in literary analysis
Study this if you are drawn to a career in publishing, creative industries, journalism, content or teaching, where the combination of writing skill and literary knowledge is directly applicable

Think twice if you are hoping the degree will make you a published novelist within three years — Creative Writing develops craft and analytical thinking, but publication requires persistence, resilience and luck that no degree can guarantee

dramaTheatre.title: Drama & Theatre Studies BA
dramaTheatre.overview: Drama & Theatre Studies is the academic and practical study of theatre as an art form, cultural practice and social force. Unlike drama school training, which focuses primarily on acting technique for professional performance, a university Drama degree combines practical theatre-making with rigorous historical, theoretical and critical study — asking why theatre matters, how it works as a medium, how it has changed across history and cultures, and what it can do that other art forms cannot. Students will act, direct, design and produce theatre in collaboration with peers, while also writing critical essays, studying dramatic texts from Sophocles to Sarah Kane and engaging with theatre theory from Brecht and Artaud to contemporary feminist and postdramatic thought. Graduates go into performance, theatre-making, education, arts administration, journalism, community arts and the wider creative economy.

yearByYear.focus: Theatre history, text and collaborative practice
modules: Acting Techniques: Stanislavski, Brecht and Beyond
Drama and Performance: From Ancient Greece to the Early Modern
Devised Theatre: Making Performance Collaboratively
Dramatic Text in Performance: Reading Plays as Scores
Introduction to Theatre Theory: Brecht, Artaud, Grotowski
Contemporary British Theatre: 1956 to the Present
Directing: Concept, Process and Realisation
Design for Performance: Space, Light and Scenography
World Theatre: African, Asian and Latin American Traditions
Applied Theatre: Drama in Community and Educational Settings
Postdramatic Theatre: Forced Entertainment, Rimini Protokoll, Nature Theatre
Musical Theatre: History, Form and Performance
The Theatre Industry: Production, Venues and Careers
Major Performance or Dissertation Project

skills: Collaborative theatre-making — working effectively in devising groups, production teams and creative ensembles under time and resource pressure
Critical analysis of dramatic texts and live performance, using a range of theoretical frameworks from Aristotelian dramaturgy to postdramatic theory
Directorial thinking — conceptualising how a text or devised piece can be realised for a specific space and audience
Project management of theatrical productions: coordinating creative teams, managing budgets and delivering to performance deadlines
Embodied communication and presence — the ability to express ideas physically and vocally in performance and group facilitation contexts

professionalBodies.name: Equity
professionalBodies.benefit: The UK's trade union for performing arts professionals; Drama graduates entering performance, stage management or related fields are eligible for Equity membership, which provides employment protections, pay guidance and professional support

professionalBodies.name: Theatre and Performance Research Association (TaPRA)
professionalBodies.benefit: TaPRA supports research in theatre and performance studies, running an annual conference with working groups across specialisms from applied theatre to dance and offering networking for early-career researchers

professionalBodies.name: Independent Theatre Council (ITC)
professionalBodies.benefit: The ITC represents independent theatre companies and venues across the UK; its resources on employment law, funding, touring and governance are invaluable for graduates setting up or working in small-to-medium theatre companies

careers.role: Community Arts Practitioner / Applied Theatre Facilitator

dramaTheatre.employerPerspective: Drama graduates from university programmes are not just performers — they are theatre thinkers who can produce, manage, teach and facilitate as well as perform or direct. In a sector that requires everyone to do multiple jobs simultaneously, that versatility is essential. We value the critical studies component as much as the practical: a Drama graduate who has studied Brecht and Boal is better placed to make theatre that actually says something than one who has only done acting classes. The dissertation and written work also give us confidence that they can communicate their ideas in funding applications, programme notes and grant reports.
dramaTheatre.entryRequirements: Typical offer: ABB–BBB at A-level; Drama or Theatre Studies A-level is useful but not always required — many programmes welcome English, History, Music or creative A-levels. Audition or practical workshop is required at some universities (especially those with strong performance components) but not all. GCSE: English at grade 5/C expected. A-level Drama is offered at a wide range of schools and colleges, though quality of provision varies.
dramaTheatre.furtherStudy: MA in Theatre Directing, Applied Theatre, Performance Studies, Dramaturgy, Theatre and Education or Arts Administration. PGCE for drama teaching. Professional training routes via drama school (Rose Bruford, Guildhall, RADA) for those wishing to move into professional performance or directing. MA in Cultural Policy or Arts Management for theatre management careers. PhD for academic careers in Theatre Studies.
isItForYou: Study this if you are genuinely passionate about theatre as an art form — not just performing, but also directing, designing, theorising and producing — and want a degree that develops you as a complete theatre-maker and thinker

Study this if you are considering a career in education, community arts, arts administration or theatre management alongside or instead of professional performance — the degree prepares you for all of these pathways effectively

Think twice if your sole ambition is professional acting and you do not value the academic critical component — a drama school or conservatoire training programme is likely to give you stronger industry contacts and a more focused performance technique, and many of the best drama schools do not require a prior degree
